text: The transvaginal ultrasound image shows the rectum adhered to the posterior wall of the uterus, which appears normal except for a small anterior fibroid. The ultrasound reveals puckering at the site of adhesion, indicating a challenging surgical area.

The surgeon identifies the attachment of the cyst to the uterus and begins to break the cyst wall for aspiration. The right fallopian tube is adherent to the ovary, and the surgeon carefully releases it. The surgeon separates two cysts, noting the complexity of endometriotic surgeries. The right ovary is released from the pelvic side wall, and the surgeon aspirates a larger cyst on the left side, describing it as a thick, old chocolate cyst. The ovary is released from the pelvis, and the surgeon tests tubal patency, confirming that both the right and left tubes are patent.

The surgeon inspects the anterior aspect of the uterus, observing that the bladder is adherent to it. Numerous nodules are visible in the area, indicating significant adhesions that require careful dissection.

The surgeon uses a non-absorbable suture on a straight needle to hook up the ovaries, leaving the cyst intact for later removal. The surgical focus shifts to the pouch of Douglas, where the rectum is visibly adhered to the vagina. The surgeon carefully navigates the right pararectal space, aiming to separate the rectum from the uterus by shaving nodular adhesions onto the uterus while avoiding the rectum.

The surgeon successfully releases the rectum from the posterior wall of the uterus, allowing it to descend. The next step involves locating the right ureter, which is isolated to facilitate the excision of nodules from the uterus. The surgeon uses a bisect instrument for coagulation and cutting, targeting nodules on the right utero sacral ligament.

The surgeon palpates the nodules within the pelvic cavity, using tactile feedback to differentiate between diseased and normal tissue. The excision of nodules is performed with precision, aided by the depth perception provided by three-dimensional laparoscopy.

The surgeon palpates the tissue to identify hard endometriotic nodules for removal, using a bisect instrument to coagulate and cut simultaneously. The rectum has been safely dissected away, allowing focus on the ureter, where additional nodules are identified and excised with precision aided by three-dimensional laparoscopy.
The surgeon excises endometriotic disease from the peritoneum above the ureter, carefully dissecting and removing nodules while preserving vital structures.

The surgeon tests the integrity of the rectum by performing a Michelin test, which involves placing water in the pelvis and injecting air and methylene blue. A serosal tear on the rectum is identified, prompting the surgeon to bring the torn serosal areas together for suturing.

The surgeon uses a 3-0 polyglycolic suture to close a raw area, demonstrating the ease of suturing with a 3-D laparoscopic view. The next step involves performing a cystectomy, carefully separating the cyst wall from the ovary after injecting vasopressin between them.

The surgeon performs a laparoscopic cystectomy on the left ovary, using hydrodissection to separate the cyst wall from the ovary without bleeding. Vasopressin is injected between the cyst wall and the ovary, facilitating the separation. The cyst wall is gently teased away using sharp and blunt dissection techniques, preserving as much ovarian tissue as possible to maintain the patient's fertility. The surgeon then moves to the right ovary, repeating the process with careful dissection to minimize reduction in ovarian reserve, using a bisect instrument to excise tissue at the hilum.

The surgeon completes the cystectomy and moves to the anterior wall of the uterus, where endometriotic nodules are visible on the left utero sacral ligament and bladder. Using scissors, the surgeon carefully excises the nodules to avoid injuring the bladder, while also removing disease from the round ligament.

The surgeon gently excises endometriotic disease using a bisect on the left side, then moves to the right side where the disease involves the right utero-sacral ligament and bladder. The surgeon carefully dissects and removes the nodules, avoiding diathermy near the bladder and opting for scissors to prevent damage. The excision continues to remove as much disease as possible, aiming to reduce recurrence and improve fertility outcomes.

The surgeon uses sharp dissection with a grasper to excise the disease, demonstrating precision with a 3D laparoscopic view. A small fibroid is visible in the foreground, which is enucleated and removed. The defect left by the fibroid removal is repaired using a polyglycine one suture, employing an inverting baseball stitch to close the shallow lesion.

The surgeon completes the closure of the defect with a single layer of sutures. The fibroid and nodules are removed, followed by the repair of the ovarian defect using a polyglycine 2-0 suture. The ovary is then secured to the peritoneum through an ovariopexy, leaving a suture tail of two to three centimeters. The procedure is repeated on the left side, ensuring both ovaries are repaired and hitched up.

The surgeon identifies and excises a nodule using a bisect tool. Another nodule located on top of the right ureter is carefully excised with sharp scissors to avoid using diathermy. Anti-adhesion barriers are applied throughout the pelvic area. The uterus is observed without the uterine manipulator, and it remains in a non-retroverted position at the end of the surgery.

The surgeon uses a hook and suction instrument to open the peritoneum over the sacral promontory, revealing the posterior part of the uterus and the right utero-sacral ligament. The surgeon carefully separates the rectum from the peritoneum along the right utero-sacral ligament to access the levator ani for posterior mesh placement to support the uterovaginal prolapse.

The surgeon opens the peritoneum on the right side, exposing the right utero sacral ligament. A U-shaped incision is made posterior to the uterus, and the rectum is pushed down. The surgeon clears fat tissue overlying the rectum and dissects into the left lateral pararectal space, aiming to reach the levator ani on either side. The rectum is visible in the center of the surgical field.

The surgeon performs a slow dissection on the right side, carefully avoiding the medial rectal artery while opening the right medial pararectal space. The rectum is visible in the center of the field. The dissection progresses until the levator ani muscle is exposed, with the aim to reach the right puborectalis muscle for mesh attachment. The surgeon then proceeds with a subtotal hysterectomy, removing the body of the uterus and leaving the cervix intact for mesh attachment during sacrocopopexy.

The surgeon completes the subtotal hysterectomy and releases adipose tissue to expose the rectum. A mesh is positioned for posterior support, and the first stitch is placed into the left puborectalis muscle using a non-absorbable suture. The stitch is pulled out and attached to the mesh, requiring deep access with surgical instruments.

The surgeon ties down a non-absorbable suture, placing seven or eight knots to secure the mesh. The left stitch is in place, and the surgeon proceeds to insert a stitch into the right puborectalis muscle, pulling the needle out and preparing to attach the mesh to the muscle.

The surgeon attaches the mesh to the rectum using multiple throws of non-absorbable suture, providing posterior support. The mesh is visibly well-positioned on the rectum. The surgeon then proceeds to dissect the bladder anteriorly, reaching down to the trigone to correct any cystocele. Using a 2-0 non-absorbable stitch, the mesh is attached to the dissected area, ensuring it is placed as low as possible for effective correction.

The surgeon places two additional stitches on the lateral side to attach the mesh to the vaginal wall, ensuring the mesh sits below the bladder. The stitches are placed superficially on the vaginal tissue.

The surgeon attaches the mesh to the sacral promontory using a number two non-absorbable suture, securing it to the longitudinal ligament. This completes the sacral colpopexy procedure, with the mesh visibly fixed in place.

The surgeon uses harmonic scalpel to carefully de-roof the ureteric tunnel during a radical hysterectomy, ensuring the ureter is not injured. The left uterine artery is visible, having already been cut.

The surgeon uses harmonic scalpel to de-roof the ureteric tunnel near the ascending branch of the uterine artery, exercising caution to avoid fistula formation and maintain vascular integrity. A bipolar instrument is preferred for precision in this delicate area.
The surgeon uses the ERBI BiClan, a precise smart bipolar coagulative instrument, to release tissue from the knuckle of the ureter, ensuring careful coagulation without damaging the ureter.

The surgeon uses a bipolar coagulative instrument to carefully coagulate and cut tissue near the ureter, releasing it from surrounding structures. The procedure is performed in the Yabuki space, medial to the ureter, with the aid of 3D laparoscopy for enhanced precision during this stressful part of the radical hysterectomy.

The surgeon releases the ureter and bladder tissue using harmonics, exercising caution near the ureter. The ureter has been completely de-roofed, and the surgeon prepares to move to the right side, noting the increased difficulty due to the angle of approach with instruments positioned on the left side.

The surgeon uses harmonics to de-roof the ureter from the left side, carefully releasing it from the surrounding parametrial tissue within the tunnel. The ureter is isolated at the knuckle, a critical area where it shifts and turns, requiring precise dissection to avoid accidental cutting or coagulation. The medial part of the ureter is dissected within the Yabuki space, revealing the ureter's path.

The surgeon uses scissors and diathermy to release tissues, employing harmonics for areas far from the ureter and switching to bipolar near the ureter. The bladder is being carefully separated from the cervix, and the ureter is fully dissected, facilitating the continuation of the hysterectomy.
